data.title: SEC clears Cboe, Nasdaq, NYSE to fast-track crypto ETP listings
data.description:
The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission on Thursday cleared Cboe Global Markets (BATS:CBOE), Nasdaq (NASDAQ:NDAQ) and Intercontinental Exchange's (NYSE:ICE) New York Stock Exchange to list and trade commodity-based trust shares under fast-track standards, including crypto exchange-traded products.

The measure applies so long as the exchanges "meet the requirements of the approved generic listing standards," the SEC said in a statement.

“By approving these generic listing standards, we are ensuring that our capital markets remain the best place in the world to engage in the cutting-edge innovation of digital assets," said SEC Chairman Paul Atkins. This approval helps to maximize investor choice and foster innovation by streamlining the listing process and reducing barriers to access digital asset products within America’s trusted capital markets."

The move comes after regulators said that registered exchanges can in fact facilitate the trading of certain spot crypto asset products, including margin, leverage or financed so-called retail commodity transactions. The SEC also unveiled its agenda covering proposed rules tied to the offer and sale of crypto assets to help clarify the regulatory framework.

In addition to the approval of the generic listing standards, the SEC OK'd the listing and trading of the Grayscale Digital Large Cap Fund (OTC:GDLC), as well as p.m.-settled options on the Cboe Bitcoin U.S. ETF Index and the Mini-Cboe Bitcoin U.S. ETF Index with third Friday expirations, nonstandard expirations and quarterly index expirations.

GDLC holds spot digital assets based on the CoinDesk 5 Index. The SEC's decision allows the multi-token fund to be converted into an ETF. Based on a fact sheet, GDLC holdings were mostly bitcoin (BTC-USD) and ether (ETH-USD), with respective weights of 72% and 17%.

data.title: Krispy Kreme rises again on FBI director's surprise endorsement
data.description:
After spiking Wednesday in reaction to remarks from FBI Director Kash Patel, Krispy Kreme (NASDAQ:DNUT) is again on the move, rallying another 10% Thursday, albeit on light volume.

During an appearance before the House Judiciary Committee on Wednesday in which the head of the FBI was asked about buying stocks while holding a government position, Patel answered that he simply saw a good investment opportunity in Krispy Kreme (NASDAQ:DNUT) and ON Semiconductor (ON).

“I just follow certain industries,” he said.

Patel’s seemingly benign remark launched DNUT up by 11% within minutes before surrendering a bulk of its gains later in the day, closing Wednesday’s trading session up with a gain of less than 1%.

Patel’s stake in DNUT was apparently made months ago when he bought between $15,000 and $50,000 worth of shares in the company. He also purchased between $50,000 and $100,000 worth of ON Semiconductor (ON).

The spike in DNUT was most likely fueled by its meme-like characteristics which resulted in an 83% rally over a three-day period in July. Meme stocks tend to move dramatically with little to no fundamental catalyst.

data.title: DeepSeek claims training R1 model cost less than $300,000; acknowledges A100 use
data.description:
Chinese AI startup DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) released a research paper that claimed the training cost of its R1 model was at a much lower cost than what U.S. competitors have seen.

The training of DeepSeek's reasoning-focused R1 model cost $294,000 and used slightly more than 500 Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) H800 GPUs, according to a research paper published by the startup in the journal Nature.

DeepSeek's claims about the development costs and the technology it used for R1 were questioned by several people in the technology space earlier this year. In January, Tesla (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk seemed to agree with CEO of Scale AI Alexandr Wang suggesting that China's DeepSeek had access to roughly 50,000 H100 Nvidia's chips, which they were unable talk about due to U.S. export controls.

AI-related stocks, including Nvidia, saw their market caps drop sharply after the release of R1 in January. The stocks have since recouped those losses. 

A100 use

In a supplementary paper, DeepSeek admitted for the first time that it owned Nvidia's A100 chips and had used them in preparatory stages of development.

“Regarding our research on DeepSeek-R1, we utilized the A100 GPUs to prepare for the experiments with a smaller model (30B parameters),” DeepSeek researchers wrote in supplementary information to the paper. “The results from this smaller model have been promising, which has allowed us to confidently scale up to 660B R1- Zero and R1.”

DeepSeek CEO Liang Wenfeng is listed among one of the authors of the paper.

The researchers noted that DeepSeek's R1 model incentivizes reasoning in large language models through reinforcement learning, according to the paper.

Researchers also said they utilized 64 of Nvidia's H800 GPUs to train its DeepSeek-R1-Zero model, with the process requiring approximately 198 hours. In total, 512 H800 GPUs were used, according to the researchers. DeepSeek's DeepSeek-R1-Zero model was pitted against DeepSeek-R1 in the paper as well. It took roughly 80 hours to train DeepSeek-R1, the researchers added.

Researchers showed that the reasoning abilities of LLMs can be incentivized through pure reinforcement learning, or RL, removing the need for human-labelled reasoning trajectories.

The trained model achieved superior performance on verifiable tasks such as mathematics, coding competitions, and science, technology, engineering and mathematics fields, surpassing its counterparts trained via conventional supervised learning on human demonstrations. 

RL is a machine learning method where an agent learns to make optimal decisions by interacting with an environment through trial and error.

data.title: CSX Corporation is seen as a rails winner by RBC Capital in any merger scenario
data.description:
RBC Capital Markets lifted its rating on CSX Corporation (NASDAQ:CSX) to Outperform from Sector Perform. The firm's view is that the stock is well positioned under virtually all consolidation scenarios, which include an acquisition of Union Pacific (UNP) being approved, CSX (NASDAQ:CSX) itself being acquired, or regulators blocking any deal.

However, RBC analyst Walter Spracklin and his team still believe the UNP-NSC merger will be approved, which was called the best overall scenario, with UNP targeting an early 2027 close. 

"Our view is that the potential approval of the UNP/NSC deal will give a potential acquirer of CSX much more certainty related to the regulatory process and increases the likelihood a potential acquirer reconsiders their position at that time (either of BNSF, CPKC or CN)," wrote Spracklin.

RBC Capital assigned a new price target of $39 to CSX (NASDAQ:CSX).

Shares of CSX (NASDAQ:CSX) rose 2.2% to $33.36 in late Thursday morning trading.

data.title: Roche deal to buy 89bio boosts MASH space
data.description:
Shares of companies focused on developing drugs for a liver condition called metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) traded higher on Thursday after Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) (OTCQX:RHHBF) agreed to acquire leading MASH developer 89bio (NASDAQ:ETNB) in a deal worth up to $3.5B.

While 89bio (NASDAQ:ETNB) climbed ~86%, Madrigal Pharma (MDGL), which already has an FDA-approved MASH therapy on the market, added ~3%.

Novo Nordisk (NVO), whose obesity therapy Wegovy was granted FDA label expansion for MASH last month, spiked after a real-world study indicated that its diabetes drug Ozempic outperformed Eli Lilly’s (LLY) Trulicity in cutting major cardiovascular events among Medicare patients.

Other notable gainers include Altimmune (ALT), Sagimet Biosciences (NASDAQ:SGMT), Akero Therapeutics (AKRO), Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR), Terns Pharmaceuticals (TERN), and Enanta Pharmaceuticals (ENTA).

Meanwhile, SPDR S&P Biotech ETF (XBI), which represents biotech stocks, gained ~2%, extending its three-month rise to ~16% compared to the ~10% jump in the S&P 500 (SP500) as the sector witnessed the latest M&A deal.

The all-cash transaction expected to close in Q4 2025 is the second major biotech deal for September, following Novartis’ (NVS) recent agreement to acquire heart drug developer Tourmaline Bio (TRML) for $1.4B early this month.

data.title: Amicus upgraded to Buy at Needham banking on kidney disease therapy
data.description:
Amicus Therapeutics (NASDAQ:FOLD) was upgraded to Buy from Hold at Needham with a $14 target, emphasizing that the company's kidney disease program, DMX-200, is undervalued.

The stock of the company is up about 5% today.

"Despite DMX-200 being a Phase III program, with US peak sales potential of >$1.5B, the opportunity in FSGS does not appear to be baked into FOLD's valuation," Needham analysts said.

In addition to the underappreciated value of DMX-200, Needham also sees upside in Amicus’s commercial Fabry disease drug, Galafold. 

Analysts at Needham said that despite being on the market for nearly a decade, Galafold continues to deliver double-digit yearly sales growth driven by rising global diagnosis rates for Fabry disease, which have nearly doubled since 2016.

data.title: David Tepper: Cutting beyond 50-75 bp could lead to ‘weaker dollar, more inflation’
data.description:
Billionaire investor David Tepper expressed a cautiously optimistic view on the Federal Reserve’s recent interest rate cut, suggesting that moderate easing won’t significantly impact the economy.

Tepper, who is the founder and president of Appaloosa Management and owner of the Carolina Panthers, believes the Fed could implement up to three rate cuts without major consequences, during an interview with CNBC.

“One easing, or two easings, or even three easings…just don’t matter.” He explained that even with these cuts, monetary policy would remain “a little restrictive” given current inflation levels.

He said that the market is already anticipating additional easing measures but warned against pushing rate cuts too aggressively.

Tepper described his current market outlook as mixed, noting that “I’m constructive because of the easing right now, but I’m also miserable because of the [valuation] levels.” He highlighted that stocks across the board are not cheap by historical standards, with the broader market trading at approximately 20x earnings.

On potential risks, Tepper cautioned that excessive rate cuts beyond the initial 50-75 basis points could lead to negative consequences, including “a weaker dollar, more inflation, and those sorts of things.”

He emphasized that the Federal Reserve should be careful about how far they push monetary easing to avoid unintended economic repercussions.

The investor also touched on tariffs, sharing that while he doesn’t generally favor them, he believes 10% tariffs are not significantly disruptive to the economy.

He suggested that some protective measures might be beneficial in an AI-driven world where employment patterns are changing, though he expressed opposition to higher tariff rates of 25% or 50%, which he characterized as “a little bit destructive.”

  • Bank of America CEO says Fed needs to be mindful of the 'inflation picture'

data.description:

data.title: Exxon has 'no plans' to re-enter Russia, CEO Woods tells FT
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (NYSE:XOM) has no plans to return to working in Russia, CEO Darren Woods told the Financial Times on Thursday, despite efforts by the Russian and U.S. governments to link energy investments to a peace deal in Ukraine.

"We don't have any plans to re-enter Russia," Woods told FT in an interview, adding that Exxon (NYSE:XOM) executives are involved in talks with Russian officials about recouping $4.6B in assets expropriated by the country but not about investing there.

Woods said the talks began in early 2023, shortly after the company filed an arbitration case against the Russian government, which had seized control of Exxon’s (XOM) 30% stake in the huge Sakhalin-1 oil project.

Analysts say Russia is eager to attract western oil majors to provide investment and technical skills that are needed for complex offshore operations such as Sakhalin-1, but the western companies are hesitant to return to Russia after writing off billions of dollars following Russia's invasion of Ukraine.

Senior Exxon (XOM) executives reportedly held talks with Russian officials last month about returning to the Sakhalin-1 project as part of a wider push by the Trump administration to secure a peace deal in Ukraine, following President Putin's decision to amend the Russian decree that seized Exxon's (XOM) Sakhalin stake, potentially paving the way for the return of western oil firms.

data.title: PayPal-, Mastercard-backed Pine Labs targets up to $700M from IPO - report
data.description:
Pine Labs, a digital payments company in India, expects to raise as much as $700M from an initial public offering in the latter half of October, according to a media report.

The company, which is backed by U.S. payment giants PayPal Holdings (NASDAQ:PYPL) and Mastercard (NYSE:MA), has started roadshows for the planned offering, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the matter. It has reduced the expected size of the IPO from an earlier target of $1B after existing investors decided to offer few shares.

The IPO is taking place as the fintech space in India benefits from the government's push into digitization. Deliberations are still taking place and details, including the IPO size and timing, could change, Bloomberg said.
